solution: 
```cpp
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <set>
#include <utility>

class SORTracker {
private:
    std::set<std::pair<int, std::string>, std::greater<>> bestLocations;
    std::vector<std::pair<int, std::string>> queryResults;
    int queryCount = 0;

public:
    SORTracker() {
    }
    
    void add(std::string name, int score) {
        bestLocations.insert({score, name});
    }
    
    std::string get() {
        if (queryCount < bestLocations.size()) {
            auto it = bestLocations.begin();
            std::advance(it, queryCount);
            queryResults.push_back(*it);
            queryCount++;
            return queryResults.back().second;
        }
        return "";
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N log N) for add, O(N) for get
space complexity: O(N)
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution uses a set to maintain locations sorted by score and name. The `add` method inserts a new location in O(log N) time. The `get` method retrieves the i-th best location by traversing the set, which takes O(N) time in the worst case. The approach is efficient for maintaining sorted order but may be slower for frequent `get` calls due to linear traversal.
